questions.question: Derive the relation between speed, frequency, and wavelength of sound.
questions.answer: Speed of sound v = frequency × wavelength, i.e., v = fλ. Derived from the definition of wavelength as distance travelled per vibration and frequency as vibrations per second.

questions.question: Explain Doppler effect and give its applications.
questions.answer: Doppler effect is the apparent change in frequency of sound due to relative motion of source and observer. Applications: speed detection by radar guns, astronomy (star motion), medical imaging.

questions.question: Derive the expression for the speed of sound in a gas in terms of its temperature.
questions.answer: v = √(γRT/M), where γ = adiabatic index, R = gas constant, T = temperature (K), M = molar mass. Shows speed increases with temperature.

questions.question: Explain formation of beats when two sounds of nearly equal frequencies interfere.
questions.answer: Two sound waves of slightly different frequencies superpose. Amplitude varies periodically, producing loud and soft sounds alternately. Beat frequency = |f1−f2|.

questions.question: Explain reverberation time and its calculation in a hall.
questions.answer: Reverberation time = time for sound to decay 60 dB after source stops. Calculated using Sabine formula: T = 0.161 V/A, V = volume, A = total absorption.

questions.question: Explain the principle and working of echo-sounding used in submarines.
questions.answer: Ultrasound is emitted from submarine. Reflected from underwater objects. Time of echo used to calculate distance: d = vt/2.

questions.question: A sound of frequency 1024 Hz travels through air at 20°C. Find its wavelength.
answer_outline: Speed of sound in air at 20°C

questions.question: Explain natural frequency and forced vibration with examples.
questions.answer: Natural frequency: frequency at which object vibrates freely (e.g., tuning fork). Forced vibration: vibration caused by external periodic force (e.g., speaker causing nearby objects to vibrate).

questions.question: Explain the difference between fundamental tone and overtones.
questions.answer: Fundamental tone: lowest frequency produced, determines pitch. Overtones: higher frequencies produced simultaneously, determine quality (timbre).

questions.question: Explain resonance in air columns and its applications in musical instruments.
questions.answer: Resonance occurs when frequency of source matches natural frequency of air column, amplifying sound. Example: organ pipes, flute, tuning of musical instruments.

questions.question: Explain how bats use ultrasound for navigation.
questions.answer: Bats emit ultrasound, reflected waves give information about objects and prey. Time delay and frequency shift help determine distance and motion.

questions.question: Explain why sound is faster in water than in air.
questions.answer: Water particles are closer and more elastic than air, allowing faster vibration transfer, increasing speed of sound.
answer_outline: Particle density and elasticity in water

questions.question: A tuning fork of 256 Hz produces beats of 4 beats/s when sounded with another fork. Find the frequency of the second fork.
Explain the formation of a sonic boom.
questions.answer: When an object moves faster than the speed of sound, it compresses air to form shock waves. This sudden release produces a loud boom called a sonic boom.

questions.question: Explain the effect of temperature and pressure on speed of sound in air.
questions.answer: Speed increases with temperature (v = 331 + 0.6T). Pressure has negligible effect because both density and elasticity change proportionally in ideal gases.

questions.question: Explain the difference between compressions and rarefactions in a sound wave.
questions.answer: Compressions: regions of high particle density and pressure. Rarefactions: regions of low particle density and pressure. Alternating compressions and rarefactions form longitudinal sound waves.

questions.question: Explain how noise pollution affects human health and methods to reduce it.
questions.answer: Noise pollution causes stress, hearing loss, sleep disturbance. Reduced using sound barriers, earplugs, zoning of industrial areas.

questions.question: Explain the use of resonance in tuning musical instruments.
questions.answer: String or air column is adjusted so its natural frequency matches desired note. Resonance amplifies the note for accurate tuning.

questions.question: Explain the principle of echolocation in submarines using ultrasound.
questions.answer: Submarine emits ultrasound. Echo reflected from object returns after time t. Distance calculated as d = vt/2. Used for mapping and object detection underwater.

questions.question: A sound travels 660 m in 2 s. Find its speed.
questions.answer: Speed v = distance/time = 660/2 = 330 m/s

questions.question: Explain how the pitch of a sound produced by a string depends on its length, tension, and mass per unit length.
questions.answer: Pitch ∝ √(tension/linear density)/length. Shorter, tighter, lighter strings produce higher pitch. Longer, looser, heavier → lower pitch.

questions.question: Explain why ultrasound is preferred over audible sound in medical imaging.
questions.answer: Ultrasound has short wavelength → better resolution. Not audible → no discomfort. Can penetrate body tissues and reflect from organs for imaging.

questions.question: Explain why sound intensity decreases with distance.
questions.answer: Sound spreads over larger area as it travels. Energy per unit area decreases → intensity decreases. Follows inverse square law.

questions.question: Explain the factors affecting loudness of sound.
questions.answer: Depends on amplitude, distance from source, sensitivity of ear, and frequency. Higher amplitude → louder sound.

questions.question: Explain the working principle of a microphone and its types.
questions.answer: Microphone converts sound waves into electrical signals using diaphragm vibrations. Types: dynamic (coil in magnetic field), condenser (capacitor plates), piezoelectric (crystal).
